food_name: Sambal Belacan (Fresh Chilli with Toasted Shrimp Paste Condiment)
food_subgroup: Fresh pounded chilli with toasted shrimp paste (condiment)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Sambal Belacan. Closest parent is fermented shrimp-based chilli condiment. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chilli condiment with fermented shrimp paste (sambal belacan/terasi style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A11EA is the closest FoodEx2 parent for chilli-based condiments; no exact code exists for sambal belacan specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for sambal belacan as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical condiment serving of sambal belacan is approximately 15g (one tablespoon), as used alongside a main meal in Malay cuisine.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal Belacan — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for sambal belacan, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Malaysian composition data. Values represent a typical fresh-pounded sambal belacan preparation. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are enriched estimates from cross-referenced composition data (confidence 0.9).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index estimation for low-carbohydrate chilli-based condiment; analogous reference: chilli sauce / condiment category, University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; MyHealth Ministry of Health Malaysia (supporting reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal belacan in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with low-carbohydrate chilli condiments and fermented shrimp-based sauces. Sambal belacan contains only 10.5g carbohydrate per 100g and is consumed in 15g condiment portions (1.6g carbohydrate per serving), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 0.2 per serving — nutritionally negligible. Estimation is consistent with MyHealth Ministry of Health Malaysia guidance. Confidence: 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content from fermented shrimp paste (belacan): 2,200 mg sodium per 100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit sambal belacan to no more than 10–15g per meal (delivering 220–330 mg sodium) and account for this within their total daily sodium budget of under 2,000 mg; heavy use of 30–50g per meal can contribute 660–1,100 mg sodium from this condiment alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.2) and capsaicin-mediated insulin sensitivity improvement
type2_diabetes.guidance: A standard 15g serving of sambal belacan contributes only 1.6g carbohydrate with a negligible glycemic load of approximately 0.2, making it safe for blood glucose management; capsaicin from fresh bird's eye chillies has been shown in clinical studies to improve insulin sensitivity and attenuate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content raises cardiovascular risk at large serving sizes; low saturated fat and beneficial capsaicin and vitamin C content are mitigating factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium density (2,200 mg/100g) poses a cardiovascular risk if sambal belacan is consumed in large quantities; however, at a standard 15g condiment serving the saturated fat is low (0.12g), and the capsaicin and vitamin C content may confer modest cardioprotective benefits, so moderate use within a sodium-controlled diet is acceptable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Capsaicin anti-inflammatory properties (TRPV1 receptor modulation) and vitamin C content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Capsaicin in fresh chilli modulates TRPV1 receptors with documented anti-inflammatory effects; a 15g serving provides approximately 11.25 mg vitamin C, and small amounts of sambal belacan are unlikely to exacerbate inflammation, though the high sodium content warrants caution at large serving sizes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Belacan is the quintessential Malay and Southeast Asian table condiment — a raw, freshly pounded chilli relish made by combining toasted belacan (dried fermented shrimp paste), fresh red bird's eye chillies, calamansi lime juice, sugar, and an optional pinch of salt, traditionally prepared using a stone mortar and pestle (cobek or lesung batu). The toasting of belacan before pounding is a critical step that transforms the raw, pungent shrimp paste into a rich, fragrant, deeply savoury ingredient with complex umami depth. The resulting condiment is intensely spicy, pungent, sour, and savoury simultaneously. Sambal belacan is served alongside virtually every Malay meal — from nasi padang and lontong to ikan bakar and nasi lemak — and functions as a dipping sauce for raw vegetables (ulam), a flavour base for stir-fried dishes such as Sambal Kangkong, and a universal table condiment. Nutritionally, it is notable for its very high sodium content (2,200 mg/100g) derived from the fermented shrimp paste, significant vitamin C (75 mg/100g) from fresh chillies, moderate protein (7.5g/100g) from the shrimp paste, and meaningful vitamin A (125 mcg RAE/100g). The glycemic load per standard 15g serving is negligible (approximately 0.2), making it safe for diabetic individuals in typical condiment quantities. Capsaicin content from bird's eye chillies confers documented anti-inflammatory and insulin-sensitising properties. The primary clinical concern is sodium: heavy users consuming 30–50g per meal may ingest 660–1,100 mg sodium from this condiment alone, which is significant for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients.
common_user_queries: how much sodium in sambal belacan
meal_context: virtually all Malay and Nanyang Chinese meals

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. The food is a well-documented regional condiment with consistent composition data across both approved sources. Minor enrichment applied for copper, manganese, and vitamin K (confidence 0.9 for those fields). GI is an analogous estimate with documented basis.
notes: Sodium content of 2,200 mg/100g is confirmed from belacan (fermented shrimp paste) composition and is nutritionally accurate for this condiment. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per source data; sambal belacan contains no hydrogenated fats by preparation. Vitamin D is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant and seafood-based condiment with no vitamin D-contributing ingredients. Portions in the input contained duplicates (two entries for 10g and two for 15g); deduplicated to one 10g and one 15g serving, with 15g tablespoon as default. Copper (0.08 mg), manganese (0.15 mg), and vitamin K (4.5 mcg) are enriched estimates from cross-referenced composition data (confidence 0.9). GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for sambal belacan in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 15g serving = (15 × 10.5 / 100) × 15 / 100 = 0.24, rounded to 0.2. This is a Singaporean/Malaysian/Indonesian dish; multilingual names for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Indonesian are all provided per G6 rules.
